**Tokyo Prefecture** (東京府, *Tōkyō-fu*) was a [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") of Japan that existed between 1868 and 1943.[[1]](#cite_note-nepyo-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Prefecture_(1868%E2%80%931943)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Tokyo_Prefectural_Office_and_Tokyo_City_Hall_1930s.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Prefectural_Office_and_Tokyo_City_Hall_1930s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Prefectural_Office_and_Tokyo_City_Hall_1930s.jpg)

Tokyo Prefectural Office and Tokyo City Hall, 1930s

When the prefecture was established with the merger of the two [shogunate city administrations](/wiki/Machi-bugy%C5%8D "Machi-bugyō") in the [Meiji restoration](/wiki/Meiji_restoration "Meiji restoration") in 1868, Tokyo initially consisted only of the former city area of the shogunate capital [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo"). Beginning in 1871, the territory of Tokyo was expanded beyond Edo in several steps to reach roughly its present extent with the [Tama transfer](/wiki/Western_Tokyo "Western Tokyo") in 1893. The surrounding [former shogunate domain](/wiki/Tenry%C5%8D "Tenryō") (incl. hatamoto fiefs) in Musashi province was initially administered by Musashi governors, but then split up between the prefectures [Shinagawa](/w/index.php?title=Shinagawa_prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shinagawa prefecture (page does not exist)"), [Kosuge](/w/index.php?title=Kosuge_prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kosuge prefecture (page does not exist)") and [Ōmiya/Urawa](/w/index.php?title=Urawa_prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Urawa prefecture (page does not exist)"). In 1871/72, the surrounding rural areas from these three prefectures and the Setagaya exclave of [Hikone ex-domain/prefecture](/wiki/Hikone_Domain "Hikone Domain") were merged into Tokyo.

The "system of large and small/major and minor districts" (大区小区制, *daiku-shōku-sei*) which was tied to the modernized family registration system *([koseki](/wiki/Koseki "Koseki"))* created an (unpopular) subdivision of all prefectures into numbered subunits.

Tokyo's administrative structure between [1871/72](/wiki/Koseki "Koseki") and 1878  
(not substantially different from other prefectures)

| Level of government (executive, legislature) | Organization in Tokyo |
| --- | --- |
| Empire (appointed [council](/wiki/Daj%C5%8Dkan "Dajōkan"),  no assembly) | [Home Ministry](/wiki/Home_Ministry_(Japan) "Home Ministry (Japan)") |
| Prefecture (appointed [governor](/wiki/Governor_(Japan) "Governor (Japan)"), no assembly) | *Tōkyō-fu* ("Tokyo Prefecture") |
|  | [numbered] large/major districts *(daiku)* & small/minor districts *(shōku)* |

In 1878, the ancient ritsuryō districts were reactivated as administrative units in rural areas, and the status of urban districts *(-ku)* was newly introduced for major cities. Under the *gunkuchōson-hensei-hō* (郡区町村編制法, "Law on the organization of -gun/-ku/-chō/-son"), both urban and rural districts were further subdivided into urban and rural units (*-machi* and *-mura*, i.e. towns and villages in the countryside, but neighbourhood-sized units in larger settlements; for example, there were 13 -machi/-chō and 93 -mura in [Ebara District](/w/index.php?title=Ebara_District,_Tokyo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ebara District, Tokyo (page does not exist)") in the 1870s, including five (one "North", three "South", one "New") for [Shinagawa](/wiki/Shinagawa-juku "Shinagawa-juku") alone; the >100 subdivisions of Ebara were merged into only 1 town and 18 villages in 1889, today there are only four special wards left in its former territory: [Shinagawa](/wiki/Shinagawa,_Tokyo "Shinagawa, Tokyo"), [Meguro](/wiki/Meguro,_Tokyo "Meguro, Tokyo"), [Ōta](/wiki/%C5%8Cta,_Tokyo "Ōta, Tokyo"), [Setagaya](/wiki/Setagaya,_Tokyo "Setagaya, Tokyo")). Initially, Tokyo contained only six [rural] districts, but other rural areas were added to Tokyo later (Izu & Ogasawara islands 1878/80, the three Tama districts 1893).

Tokyo's administrative structure between [1878](/wiki/Districts_of_Japan "Districts of Japan") and 1889

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Empire (appointed [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan") from 1885, no assembly) | Home Ministry | |
| Prefecture (appointed governor, elected [assembly](/w/index.php?title=Prefectural_assembly_(Japan)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prefectural assembly (Japan) (page does not exist)")) | *Tōkyō-fu* ("Tokyo Prefecture") | |
| District (appointed chief, no *(gun)*/elected *(ku)* assembly) | 15 [urban] districts (*-ku*) | 6 [rural] districts (*-gun*) |
| Locality/[Proto-]Municipality (with restrictions: elected chief, elected [assembly](/w/index.php?title=Municipal_assembly_(Japan)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Municipal assembly (Japan) (page does not exist)") from 1880) | hundreds of urban and rural subdivisions/neighbourhoods, towns and villages (*-machi/-chō* and *-mura*) | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/bb/Modern_Japan_prefectures_map_in_1872.jpg)](/wiki/File:Modern_Japan_prefectures_map_in_1872.jpg)

Tokyo on a map of 1872 prefectures, bordering [Inba](/w/index.php?title=Inba_Prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Inba Prefecture (page does not exist)") in the East, [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture") and [Iruma](/w/index.php?title=Iruma_Prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Iruma Prefecture (page does not exist)") in the North, and [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa "Kanagawa") in the West and South

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Map_tokyo_3tama_p01-01.png/250px-Map_tokyo_3tama_p01-01.png)](/wiki/File:Map_tokyo_3tama_p01-01.png)

The "three Tama" (三多摩, *san-Tama*) districts transferred from Kanagawa to Tokyo in 1893: [West Tama](/wiki/Nishitama_District,_Tokyo "Nishitama District, Tokyo") in orange, [North Tama](/wiki/Kitatama_District,_Tokyo "Kitatama District, Tokyo") in yellow, [South Tama](/wiki/Minamitama_District,_Tokyo "Minamitama District, Tokyo") in green.

When the modern municipalities were introduced in 1889, Tokyo was subdivided into c. 80 municipalities: 1 city, a handful of towns, and dozens of villages. With the Tama transfer of 1893, the number of municipalities in Tokyo grew to over 170. By 1943, there were only 87 municipalities left: 3 cities, 18 towns and 66 villages (see the [List of mergers in Tokyo](/wiki/List_of_mergers_in_Tokyo "List of mergers in Tokyo")).

Tokyo's administrative structure between [1889](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Japan "Municipalities of Japan") and 1943 (not different from Osaka, Kyoto)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Empire (appointed cabinet, [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Japan) "Imperial Diet (Japan)") with two equal chambers: [one appointed](/wiki/House_of_Peers_(Japan) "House of Peers (Japan)"), [one elected](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)")) | Home Ministry | | | |
| Prefecture (appointed governor & ["council"](/w/index.php?title=Sanjikai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sanjikai (page does not exist)"), elected assembly) | *Tōkyō-fu* ("Tokyo Prefecture") | | | |
| (District) (appointed chief & ["council"](/w/index.php?title=Sanjikai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sanjikai (page does not exist)"), indirectly elected assembly) | *Tōkyō-shi* ("[Tokyo City](/wiki/Tokyo_City "Tokyo City")") (until 1898 without independent administration: pref. governor=city mayor) | 0→2 other cities (*-shi*) Hachiōji 1917, Tachikawa 1940 | 6→9→8→3 Districts (*-gun*) (until abolition in 1920s) | Subprefectures (for island municipalities) |
| Municipality ([mayor](/w/index.php?title=Mayor_(Japan)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mayor (Japan) (page does not exist)") appointed from assembly proposals + in cities: ["council"](/w/index.php?title=Sanjikai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sanjikai (page does not exist)") from 1920s: indirectly elected mayor [& "council"], elected assembly) | >80→>170→84 towns (*-machi*) and villages (*-mura*) | |
|  | 15→35 [Wards](/wiki/Wards_of_Japan "Wards of Japan") (*-ku*) |

Even after the Tama transfer, Tokyo City remained the dominant part of Tokyo in terms of population and economic strength. That increased further during the progressing industrialization and the explosive growth of the city in the early 20th century, only temporarily set back by the devastation brought about by the 1923 Great Kantō earthquake. The outskirts grew, but eventually Tokyo City's dominance within Tokyo only increased again as many of the explosively grown suburbs were merged into Tokyo City in 1932, including some of the largest [towns](/wiki/Towns_of_Japan "Towns of Japan") in Japanese history with over 100,000 inhabitants each such as [Nishisugamo](/w/index.php?title=Nishisugamo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nishisugamo (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E5%B7%A3%E9%B4%A8 "ja:西巣鴨")] in [Kitatoshima District](/w/index.php?title=Kitatoshima_District&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kitatoshima District (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%8C%97%E8%B1%8A%E5%B3%B6%E9%83%A1 "ja:北豊島郡")] and [Shibuya](/w/index.php?title=Shibuya_(town)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shibuya (town) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B8%8B%E8%B0%B7%E7%94%BA_(%E6%9D%B1%E4%BA%AC%E5%BA%9C) "ja:渋谷町 (東京府)")] in [Toyotama District](/w/index.php?title=Toyotama_District&action=edit&redlink=1 "Toyotama District (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%B1%8A%E5%A4%9A%E6%91%A9%E9%83%A1 "ja:豊多摩郡")].

Various plans for a unification of the prefectural and city government were discussed over the decades. An early proposal in the 1890s by then Home Minister [Nomura Yasushi](/wiki/Nomura_Yasushi "Nomura Yasushi") envisioned to separate the rural areas of Tokyo as Musashi prefecture and transform only Tokyo City into a "Metropolis", but it failed in the Imperial Diet. Some plans, especially those by the commoner political parties and during the "Taishō democracy" of the 1920s, envisioned a "Metropolis" more similar to a [special city](/w/index.php?title=Special_cities_of_Japan_(1947%E2%80%931953)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Special cities of Japan (1947–1953) (page does not exist)"): an enlarged, prefecture-level city with *more* local autonomy. While the city did gain some additional authority under the 1922 "six major cities law" (more formally: 六大都市行政監督ニ関スル法律, *roku-daitoshi gyōsei kantoku ni kan suru hōritsu*, "Law relating to the administrative supervision of the six major cities"), and the governments made plans for a "Metropolis" system – the 1932 "Greater Tokyo City" mergers had been part of a Metropolis plan from the Tokyo City Assembly –, the actual reform was carried out later as part of the Tōjō cabinet's wartime authoritarian centralization measures (or "simplification of local government"). Not only was the Home Ministry control over prefectures and municipalities tightened as in the whole country – municipal mayors became appointive similar to the Meiji era –; Tokyo's prefectural government and Tokyo City's municipal government were indeed unified into one "Metropolitan" government, but under still tighter central government supervision.

Thus, in 1943, 86 of Tokyo's 87 municipalities remained Tokyo's municipalities, [Tokyo City](/wiki/Tokyo_City "Tokyo City") was abolished, all municipalities and the 35 ex-city wards were now part of [Tokyo Metropolis](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") (東京都, *Tōkyō-to*) which continues to serve as prefectural government for all of Tokyo, but now additionally as the municipal government in former Tokyo City. The governor of Tokyo, previously *chiji* as in all prefectures, was now called *chōkan* ("head/chief" [often of a central government agency]) and tied even more closely to the Imperial government than the governors of other prefectures. He became a *shinninkan* (親任官), meaning he was appointed directly by the Emperor, [in the same procedure](/wiki/Shinninshiki#Empire_of_Japan "Shinninshiki") as a member of the Cabinet, the governors of Chōsen/Korea or Taiwan/Formosa, or an Army General or Navy Admiral.

Tokyo's wartime administrative structure from [1943](/wiki/T%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_Cabinet "Tōjō Cabinet") (unique)

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Empire (appointed cabinet, bicameral appointed/elected Diet) | Emperor/Cabinet/Home Ministry | | |
| Prefecture (appointed governor, elected assembly) | *Tōkyō-to* ("Tokyo Metropolis") | | |
| Municipality (appointed mayor, elected assembly) |  | 2 cities (*-shi*) | 84 towns (*-machi*) and villages (*-mura*) |
|  | 35 [wards](/wiki/Wards_of_Japan "Wards of Japan") (*-ku*) |

The "Metropolis" is not to be confused with the [Tokyo metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") which extends into prefectures other than Tokyo and, depending on definition, may or may not include all of the "Metropolis".

In 1944/45, the establishment of regional bureaus created new parallel local administrative structures, lacking even the limited control by elected assemblies that prefectures and municipalities featured. And on the local level, the pre-existing neighbourhood associations (see *[chōnaikai](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dnaikai "Chōnaikai")* and *[Tonarigumi](/wiki/Tonarigumi "Tonarigumi")*) had been tied into the totalitarian Yokusankai vision and were endowed with far-reaching authority to establish an authoritarian system of control reaching down even to individual citizens. But the war tide had turned, and soon, the occupation under Douglas MacArthur overturned the wartime centralization, and beyond that, introduced new far-reaching local autonomy rights for prefectures, municipalities and even citizens in the form of "direct demands" (*chokusetsu seikyū*: recalls, popular initiative referendums for prefectural/municipal by-laws [excluding taxation], petitions, etc.).

The title *chōkan* for the governor actually remained in place until 1947 when the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") and the [Local Autonomy Law](/wiki/Local_Autonomy_Law "Local Autonomy Law") made Tokyo equal with other prefectures again and gave the residents of former Tokyo City (almost) the same rights as in other municipalities with the introduction of [special wards](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo"). The [first gubernatorial election](/w/index.php?title=1947_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election&action=edit&redlink=1 "1947 Tokyo gubernatorial election (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/1947%E5%B9%B4%E6%9D%B1%E4%BA%AC%E9%83%BD%E9%95%B7%E5%AE%98%E9%81%B8%E6%8C%99 "ja:1947年東京都長官選挙")], held in April 1947 as part of the [1st unified elections](/w/index.php?title=1947_unified_local_elections_in_Japan&action=edit&redlink=1 "1947 unified local elections in Japan (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%AC%AC1%E5%9B%9E%E7%B5%B1%E4%B8%80%E5%9C%B0%E6%96%B9%E9%81%B8%E6%8C%99 "ja:第1回統一地方選挙")], was still held as *Tōkyō-to chōkan senkyo*, and the first elected governor (who had also been the penultimate appointed governor from 1946 to 1947) initially still took office as *chōkan*, but became *chiji* in May 1947.[[1]](#cite_note-nepyo-1)
